characters.description: Avar warrior and former Shamil naib who has defected to the Russians; narrates his life story to Loris-Melikov across chapters 11-13, awaits news of his family in Tiflis.
Vorontsov's aide-de-camp, fluent in Tartar, who interviews Hadji Murad and records his biography; assigned to accompany him to Grozny.
Viceroy of the Caucasus who writes a lengthy French-language report to Chernyshov on 20 December about how to handle Hadji Murad; gave Hadji Murad a repeater watch as a gift.
Referenced only as the woman captivated by Hadji Murad's childlike smile.
Hadji Murad's eldest brother and foster-brother to Abu Nutsal Khan; killed by Hamzad's murids during the assassination of Hamzad in the mosque.
Hadji Murad's second brother, who died in infancy.
Hadji Murad's mother; refused to wet-nurse Bulach Khan and was wounded by his father with a dagger; composed a song about the event; now in Shamil's hands.
Passionate man who struck Hadji Murad's mother with a dagger when she refused to wet-nurse again.
Eldest of the three Avar Khans of Khunzakh, foster-brother to Osman; killed by Hamzad's murids after being lured to his tent.

aliases: the second son of the Khan
characters.description: Second Avar Khan, nursed by Hadji Murad's mother, strong but weak-willed; lost money gambling with Russian officers in Tiflis; killed by Hamzad's murids.
Youngest Avar Khan sent as hostage to Hamzad; later thrown over a precipice by Shamil.
Mother of the three Avar Khans; sent her sons to their deaths with Hamzad; beheaded on Hamzad's orders by the murid Aseldar.
First Imam mentioned in Hadji Murad's backstory, killed before the events of the chapter.
Second Imam of Dagestan, successor to Kazi-Mulla; lured and murdered the Avar Khans; assassinated in a mosque by Hadji Murad and his brother Osman.
Russian Commander-in-Chief at Tiflis who refused to receive Hadji Murad and Umma Khan and failed to deliver promised help; later sent Hadji Murad an officer's commission.
Imam of Chechnya and Dagestan, successor to Hamzad; Hadji Murad's rival who holds his family hostage and seeks his death.
Hadji Murad's one-eyed red-haired murid who loathes Russians and still reveres Shamil; mistrusted by Loris-Melikov.
Hadji Murad's cheerful, reckless murid; gambled 12 kopecks into gold coins in Tiflis; ready to serve either side for the thrill.
Young, strong, devoted murid of Hadji Murad; described as firm and 'made of iron'.
Hairy, strong murid who manages Hadji Murad's household; adopted as 'brother' by Patimat after his father killed Hadji Murad's uncle.
Earlier saintly Imam referenced admiringly by Khan Mahoma in contrast to Shamil.
Hamzad's favorite murid who beheaded the Khansha; killed by Hadji Murad during the mosque assassination.
Daughter of the Avar Khansha whose refused marriage triggered Akhmet Khan's hatred of Hadji Murad.
Briefly appointed Khan of Kazi-Kumukh by Rosen before Akhmet Khan replaced him.
Khan of Kazi-Kumukh who hated Hadji Murad over the Sultanetta marriage refusal; slandered him to Klugenau and had him arrested and chained.
Russian general who initially defended Hadji Murad and wrote two letters urging him to return; his letters are preserved and shown to Loris-Melikov.
Russian Minister of War, a parvenu who resents Vorontsov and tries to present his handling of Hadji Murad unfavorably to the Tsar.
Autocratic Emperor of Russia, portrayed as vain, cruel, and sexually predatory; receives Vorontsov's report and endorses a brutal Caucasian policy.
Russian general mentioned in Vorontsov's letter as recipient of instructions regarding Hadji Murad.
Russian general commanding at Grozny area who is to supervise Hadji Murad's movements.
Commander of the Shoushin District who lodged with Hadji Murad in Tiflis, spoke Tartar perfectly, and won his confidence; trusted by Vorontsov for delicate consultations.
The younger Vorontsov to whom Hadji Murad first surrendered; referred to by the Viceroy as Hadji Murad's kunak.
Decembrist relative whose conviction and property Chernyshov pursued; referenced to explain Nicholas's contempt for Chernyshov.
Assistant Minister of War with bored expression, imitating Nicholas's whiskers; present at the Emperor's audience and at dinner.
Fresh-faced young aide-de-camp with mustaches combed like the Emperor's, ushering Chernyshov into the cabinet.
Officer discovered in the theater box with his domino-clad partner; yields his seat to a furious Nicholas.
Twenty-year-old virgin, daughter of a Swedish governess, who arranged to meet Nicholas at a masquerade and spent an hour with him.
Nicholas's established mistress with powerful shoulders, glimpsed at church during the New Year service.
Enormous student of the School of Jurisprudence whom Nicholas stops on the embankment and calls a blockhead for not wishing to join the army.

aliases: student of the Academy of Medicine
characters.description: Polish Catholic medical student who stabbed his examiner with a penknife; sentenced by Nicholas to run the gauntlet of 12,000 strokes.
Medical Academy professor who failed Bzhezovski three times and received trifling knife wounds in the attack.
Grand Duchess whose carriage Nicholas sees approaching the palace; he despises her intellectual circle.
Nicholas's recently deceased brother, recalled with a feeling of sadness and vexation.
Nicholas's brother-in-law, granter of a Prussian constitution, whom Nicholas considers weak and stupid.
General-Governor who receives Nicholas's approval to court-martial Uniate peasants; submits silently to cruel orders to protect his career.
Nicholas's wife with trembling head and fixed smile, entering the Pompeian Hall for dinner.
Dinner guest who quips in French about Poland and the Caucasus being Russia's two running sores.
Dinner guest at the Pompeian Hall.
Dinner guest who feigns surprise at Lieven's remark and flatters Nicholas's strategic genius.
Silent dinner guest to whom the Ambassador had earlier confided about Nicholas's delusion of strategic greatness.
Minister of the Court, ordered by Nicholas to pay a pension to Kopervine's mother.
Unnamed ballet dancer who catches Nicholas's eye; he orders a diamond ring presented to her via the German ballet-master.
Summoned by Nicholas to convey a diamond ring to a favored ballet dancer.

fusion_hints.reason: Both are Russian generals named only in Vorontsov's chapter 14 letter as recipients of identical instructions for overseeing Hadji Murad at Grozny. Neither speaks or acts. Can be fused into a single 'Russian line commander' for the screenplay.
Mahomet-Murza is a one-line placeholder (appointed Khan of Kazi-Kumukh then replaced). Function is identical to Akhmet Khan's villain role; can collapse into Akhmet Khan without loss.

Both are early Russian generals in Hadji Murad's backstory whose authority vacillates and who fail to protect him. A single 'earlier Russian general' archetype suffices for the Tiflis-flashback compression.
All are dinner-table cameos in chapter 15 serving only to flatter Nicholas's strategic vanity. Can be compressed to one or two symbolic courtiers.
Both are named palace-courtyard glimpses that feed Nicholas's irritation; neither interacts. Fusion into a single 'reform-minded relative' reduces POV noise.
All three are silent or near-silent foils who exist to show Nicholas's capricious cruelty/contempt. Any two can merge into a single archetypal 'young officer/student Nicholas humiliates'.
Both are functionaries who execute Nicholas's whims around the ballerina and Kopervine pension. Single 'court functionary' can cover both errands.
Three women who exist solely to mark Nicholas's sexual appetite in chapter 15. Consolidating to Kopervine plus one 'established mistress' silhouette removes redundancy.

Both are devoted, silent, hard-working murids with minimal differentiating characterization (Eldar is young and handsome; Khanefi is hairy and runs the kitchen). In a 120-min feature, fusing them into one loyal foot-soldier murid is viable.
Aseldar exists only to execute Hamzad's killings (Khansha) and to be killed by Hadji Murad in the mosque. The beheading can be performed by Hamzad himself on screen to save a minor antagonist.
Akhmet is a single-line note that he died young; his only function is to underscore family mortality. Can be folded into Osman's backstory or cut.

Both Avar Khan brothers die in the same Hamzad-tent sequence performing near-identical narrative roles (lured, murdered). Can be consolidated into a single 'Avar Khan brother' to Hadji Murad, simplifying the Khunzakh backstory.
Both appear in chapter 15 as courtiers who silently execute Nicholas's cruel orders. A single 'compliant minister' can carry the Uniate-peasants court-martial beat and the pension errand.
Both are deep-backstory Imam references mentioned only to contextualize Shamil. Either can be cut; if one is retained, the other merges into the same 'earlier Imam' reference.
